title: Exercises: Scale-up and Manufacturing Modeling
description: Practice with scale-up calculations and predictions

exercises.title: Damköhler Number Analysis
exercises.description: Calculate Damköhler number at different scales.
exercises.task:
A second-order reaction has k = 0.5 L/(mol·s) at 50°C.

Lab scale (500 mL):
- Mixing time τ_mix = 2 seconds
- Initial concentration C₀ = 1.0 M

Production scale (5000 L):
- Mixing time τ_mix = 60 seconds
- Same concentration

(a) Calculate Da at lab scale
(b) Calculate Da at production scale
(c) What regime is each in? What implications for selectivity?

solution.part_a: Da = k × C × τ_reaction / τ_mix. For second order: τ_reaction = 1/(k×C). τ_rxn = 1/(0.5 × 1.0) = 2 s. Da_lab = τ_rxn / τ_mix = 2/2 = 1.0. Intermediate regime.
solution.part_b: Same k and C, but τ_mix = 60s. Da_prod = 2/60 = 0.033. Kinetically controlled regime.
solution.part_c: Lab (Da=1): Mixed regime - some transport influence but kinetics still important. Lab results should be reasonable approximation. Production (Da=0.033): Kinetically controlled. Counter-intuitively, the larger scale is MORE kinetically controlled because reaction is slow compared to mixing at production scale. If reaction were faster (k larger), Da would increase and production might become transport-limited while lab stays kinetic. In this case, scale-up should be straightforward.

hints: Da < 0.1: kinetic control, Da > 10: transport control

exercises.title: Polymorph Stability
exercises.description: Analyze polymorph stability from lattice energies.
exercises.task:
DFT-D calculations for a drug give lattice energies:

Form I: E_lattice = -42.5 kcal/mol
Form II: E_lattice = -41.8 kcal/mol
Form III: E_lattice = -40.2 kcal/mol

Solvation free energy (same for all forms): -15.0 kcal/mol
Entropy of melting estimate: 10 cal/(mol·K)
Melting points: Form I: 180°C, Form II: 165°C

(a) Rank polymorphs by thermodynamic stability
(b) Estimate relative solubility at 25°C
(c) Which form would you develop for a drug product?

solution.part_a: More negative lattice energy = more stable. Ranking: Form I (-42.5) > Form II (-41.8) > Form III (-40.2). Form I is thermodynamically most stable.
solution.part_b: Solubility ratio: S₂/S₁ = exp[(E₁ - E₂)/RT]. S(II)/S(I) = exp[(-42.5 - (-41.8))/(0.00199 × 298)] = exp[-0.7/0.593] = exp(-1.18) = 0.31. So Form II is actually LESS soluble despite being less stable - wait, let me reconsider. A weaker lattice (less negative) should be MORE soluble. S(II)/S(I) = exp[(E_II - E_I)/RT] = exp[0.7/0.593] = 3.2. Form II is 3.2× more soluble. Form III: S(III)/S(I) = exp[2.3/0.593] = 49× more soluble.
solution.part_c: Form I for drug product: Most stable, won't convert to other forms. Accept lower solubility and address with formulation (salt, particle size, ASD) rather than risk metastable form converting during storage. If solubility is critical and Form III is kinetically stable enough, it might be considered with extensive stability testing.

hints: More negative lattice energy = more stable
Consider both stability and solubility for development

exercises.title: Heat Transfer Scale-Up
exercises.description: Analyze heat transfer limitations at scale.
exercises.task:
An exothermic reaction with ΔH = -30 kcal/mol will be scaled:

Lab (1 L jacketed flask):
- Surface area: 500 cm²
- U = 100 W/(m²·K)
- ΔT_max = 30°C (jacket at 20°C, reaction at 50°C)

Production (1000 L reactor):
- Surface area: 10 m²
- U = 150 W/(m²·K) (better jacket)
- Same ΔT_max allowed

(a) Calculate maximum heat removal rate at each scale
(b) Calculate maximum reaction rate (mol/hr) at each scale
(c) What is the scale-up factor for rate vs volume?

solution.part_a: Q_max = U × A × ΔT. Lab: Q = 100 W/(m²·K) × 0.05 m² × 30 K = 150 W. Production: Q = 150 × 10 × 30 = 45,000 W = 45 kW.
solution.part_b: Heat from reaction: Q = r × ΔH. ΔH = 30 kcal/mol = 125.5 kJ/mol. Lab: r_max = 0.15 kW / 125.5 kJ/mol = 0.0012 mol/s = 4.3 mol/hr. Production: r_max = 45 / 125.5 = 0.358 mol/s = 1290 mol/hr.
solution.part_c: Volume scale-up: 1000×. Rate scale-up: 1290/4.3 = 300×. Rate scales with surface area, not volume! To match volumetric productivity, need additional cooling (internal coils, reflux, etc.) or run at lower concentration. Alternatively, switch to continuous flow for better heat transfer.

hints: Compare A/V ratios at different scales
Rate limited by heat removal, not kinetics

exercises.title: Technology Transfer Risk Assessment
exercises.description: Assess risks for site-to-site technology transfer.
exercises.task:
A pharmaceutical process is being transferred from Site A (USA) to Site B (Ireland).

Known differences:
1. Raw material supplier (different impurity profile)
2. Reactor geometry (Site B has baffled reactor)
3. Altitude (Site A: sea level, Site B: 200m)
4. Water source (different mineral content)
5. Ambient humidity (Site B more humid)

The reaction is: Reduction of ketone with NaBH4 in MeOH
Critical: Stereoselectivity (>99% ee required)

For each difference, assess:
- Risk level (High/Medium/Low)
- Mechanism of potential impact
- Recommended action

assessments.mechanism: Impurities could act as competing substrates or inhibitors. Different particle size affects dissolution rate.
assessments.action: Perform comparative testing with new supplier material. Require same specifications.

assessments.mechanism: Better mixing with baffles. For this homogeneous reaction in MeOH, mixing is not limiting. Selectivity from prochiral face attack, not mixing-dependent.
assessments.action: Confirm with small-scale comparison. Likely no impact.

assessments.mechanism: 200m difference negligible for atmospheric pressure effects. Boiling point of MeOH essentially unchanged.
NaBH4 is water-sensitive. Trace water in MeOH or carried over could affect reduction rate. Ca/Mg in water could complex with product.
assessments.action: Specify water content in MeOH. Use deionized water for any aqueous workup.

assessments.mechanism: NaBH4 degrades with moisture. Higher humidity could reduce reagent potency during handling/charging.
assessments.action: Store NaBH4 under inert atmosphere. Charge under nitrogen blanket. Verify potency before use.

solution.summary: Focus validation on: humidity control for NaBH4 handling, raw material qualification, and water content in solvents.

hints: Consider reaction mechanism for each factor
